results.title: Is there a similar substitute for Mylar? - Overgrow.com
results.content:
I work in a furniture plant and we have some arborite (laminate) material that has brushed stainless steel on the face. I have been contemplating trying some out to see if it would reflect as well as my current white paint. The brushed surface would be great for dispersing the light. The only drawback is that it is quite pricey but very durable. [...] If affordable, Lumacept and Tetra Match are two extremely reflective paints from a company in the US. The downside is the price, $200.00/gal for the Lumacept and $180.00/gal for the Tetra Match. In a communication with a company rep, he stated that the reflectivity of the two was nearly identical, but the Tetra Match was designed for indoor horticulture. The Lumacept is for settings such as hospital rooms where strong ultraviolet c is used to kill disease organisms. Both reflect visible and uv light well over 95%.

### Related topics [...] # Is there a similar substitute for Mylar?

Contributed by: Stickydank

I’ve discovered the cheapest Mylar around-- only 2 bucks$! You save 18 bucks$ a roll! It’s a Christmas present wrapping Mylar called Polywrap

 thick X 3 1/2 ft. wide X 20ft long.

Note: Only buy the very Bright and shiny Mylar-type wrapping!

I was under the impression you didn,t want a mirror like finish it has something to do with hotspots. That being said 2 bucks a roll is awesome.

if you have mylar up flat it shouldnt cause hot spots. but to be safe. you can get the reflective waterproof roof paint. its made for reflecting light from your house keeping it cooler. along with being waterproof its a win win. ive never used mylar because i have 0 patientsXD

results.title: Proofing Basket Alternative: What To Use Instead of A Banneton
results.content:
This is a double batch of sourdough bread made with 1kg of flour and placed into a large farmhouse loaf tin to proof.

## Wok

If you have a wok in your kitchen, you could use it to proof your bread in a pinch. It’s certainly not ideal, but the rounded base will support your dough.

It’s a good idea to use a thin cotton tea towel or other cloth to line the wok and wick some of the moisture away from your dough.

It also helps to lift the dough out when it’s ready to bake.

## What Cloth Should I Use?

When using a proofing basket alternative, you will more than likely need to line it with a cloth.

You should choose a thin, cotton or linen cloth. A thin linen tea towel, napkin or even pillow case are best. [...] 2.   Jenbsays: February 6, 2023 at 10:05 pm I’m sure you’ve more experience but I’ve been using parchment paper for two years without incident. Just easier (no sticking to the bowl/fabric or issues with deflation when transferring to the Dutch oven) Reply [...] Some garden centres may have a large range of pots and you will be able to find one that is shorter and wider rather than the traditional shape.

Do not attempt to bake your bread in a terracotta pot. This can transfer chemicals from the pot to your bread.

### Ricotta Basket

A ricotta basket is a great alternative to a proofing basket or banneton. You can generally find them in two different sizes at the supermarket – 500g and 1kg.

I tend to stick to the 500g as the 1kg tend to make the dough spread out too much.

Use a thin cloth to line the ricotta basket. An old, clean, cotton pillow case is perfect.

Rub it really well with rice flour to ensure the dough doesn’t stick.

You’ll find that the dough will take on the bumpy pattern from the basket.
